questions.q: In which country was Jagadish Chandra Bose born?
questions.explanation: Bose was born in 1858 in Bengal, in British India (in present-day Bangladesh).

questions.q: Jagadish Chandra Bose is best known as a pioneer in the study of which kind of waves?
questions.explanation: Bose was a pioneer in generating and studying very short radio (microwave) waves.

questions.q: Bose famously demonstrated that which living things respond to stimuli?
questions.explanation: Bose showed that plants react to stimuli such as light, touch, and injury, a then-radical idea.

questions.q: What instrument did Bose invent to measure plant growth?
questions.explanation: Bose invented the crescograph, which could magnify and record the tiny movements of plant growth.

questions.q: In which broad field did Bose work, combining physics with the study of life?
questions.explanation: Bose bridged physics and biology, making him a key early figure in biophysics.

questions.q: Bose is often described as a 'polymath.' What does that mean?
options: A person skilled in many fields
A person who studies only maths
questions.explanation: A polymath is someone whose expertise spans many subjects, which fit Bose's work in physics and botany.

questions.q: The crescograph was mainly used to study what aspect of plants?
questions.explanation: The crescograph recorded the minute, slow growth and movements of plants by magnifying them enormously.

questions.q: Bose carried out his pioneering radio-wave experiments in which city?
questions.explanation: Bose conducted his famous microwave experiments at Presidency College in Calcutta in the 1890s.

questions.q: Which honour did Bose receive from the British Crown?
questions.explanation: Bose was knighted in 1917, becoming Sir Jagadish Chandra Bose.

questions.q: Bose founded a research institute in Calcutta named after himself. What is it called?
questions.explanation: Bose founded the Bose Institute in Calcutta in 1917 to advance scientific research.

questions.q: In which year was Jagadish Chandra Bose born?
questions.explanation: Bose was born on 30 November 1858.

questions.q: At which college in Calcutta did Bose serve as a professor of physics?
questions.explanation: Bose was a professor of physics at Presidency College, Calcutta, where he did his radio research.

questions.q: At which famous university did Bose study natural sciences after leaving India?
questions.explanation: Bose studied natural sciences at Christ's College, University of Cambridge.

questions.q: Roughly how short were the radio wavelengths Bose worked with, which we now call microwaves?
questions.explanation: Bose generated very short waves of around 5 millimetres, well into the microwave range.

questions.q: Bose developed an early device to detect radio waves. What general type of detector was it?
questions.explanation: Bose built and improved the coherer, an early detector used to sense radio waves.

questions.q: Which scientific society in London elected Bose as a Fellow in 1920?
questions.explanation: Bose was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society (FRS) in 1920, a rare honour for an Indian scientist of his era.

questions.q: Bose famously chose not to do which of the following with most of his inventions?
questions.explanation: Bose was reluctant to patent his inventions, preferring to share his work freely with the world.

questions.q: Bose argued that the response of living tissue and certain inanimate materials showed which surprising similarity?
questions.explanation: Bose found that metals, plants, and animal tissue gave similar fatigue and response curves when stimulated.

questions.q: Where did Bose give a landmark 1895 public demonstration of his wireless waves?
questions.explanation: In 1895 at the Town Hall in Calcutta, Bose used radio waves to ring a bell and ignite gunpowder at a distance.

questions.q: Bose's 1895 demonstration showed radio waves could pass through walls and a person's body to do what at a distance?
options: Ring a bell and trigger an explosion
questions.explanation: His remote signalling rang a bell and set off a small charge, proving wireless transmission worked through obstacles.

questions.q: Besides physics, in which area of biology did Bose make major contributions?
questions.explanation: Bose made pioneering contributions to plant physiology, studying how plants respond to environment and stimuli.

questions.q: In which year did Jagadish Chandra Bose die?
questions.explanation: Bose died on 23 November 1937, just before his 79th birthday.

questions.q: Bose used a semiconductor crystal as a detector of radio waves. What modern device does this anticipate?
questions.explanation: Bose's use of galena and other crystals to detect waves anticipated semiconductor diodes and point-contact devices.

questions.q: Bose presented his radio research to the Royal Institution in London in 1897 in a lecture often associated with which scientist's earlier electromagnetic work?
options: James Clerk Maxwell and Heinrich Hertz
questions.explanation: Bose's work built on the electromagnetic theory of Maxwell and the wave experiments of Hertz.

questions.q: Which apparatus did Bose build to study the optical properties of his very short radio waves, mirroring tools used for light?
options: Prisms, lenses, and polarisers for microwaves
questions.explanation: Bose built waveguides, horn antennas, prisms, lenses, and polarisers to show his microwaves behaved like light.

questions.q: Bose's plant-response work led him to claim that plants possess what kind of system analogous to animals?
questions.explanation: Bose argued plants have electrical signalling resembling a nervous system, recording electrical responses to stimuli.

questions.q: Which 2009 IEEE recognition acknowledged Bose's role in early radio science?
options: He was named among the fathers of radio/wireless science
He won a Nobel Prize posthumously
He was given the Fields Medal
questions.explanation: The IEEE has credited Bose among the early pioneers of radio science, recognising his millimetre-wave work.

questions.q: Bose published a notable scientific book in 1902 dealing with which subject?
options: Response in the living and non-living
questions.explanation: His 1902 book 'Response in the Living and Non-Living' compared stimulus responses across matter and tissue.

questions.q: A lunar crater was named in honour of Bose. On which body is this crater located?
questions.explanation: A crater on the far side of the Moon is named 'Bose' in his honour.

questions.q: Bose's improved coherer and crystal detectors mark him as an early figure in which technology, decades ahead of its time?
questions.explanation: By using crystals to detect radio waves, Bose foreshadowed solid-state electronics long before transistors existed.
